coredata.dc:title: Amorphous carbon dot and chitosan based composites as fluorescent inks and luminescent films
coredata.dc:creator: Siddique, Abu Bakar
Singh, Vishwa Pratap
Pramanick, Ashit Kumar
Ray, Mallar
coredata.dc:description: A composite of self-passivated amorphous carbon dots (CDs) and chitosan has been developed and utilized to form fluorescent inks and luminescent films. The ink is invisible under visible light but glows brightly under external excitation. Cross-linking between the numerous surface groups present in the highly disordered CDs and chitosan, endow the inks and films with enhanced optical and mechanical properties. The amorphous CD based ink is capable of writing on nearly all types of surfaces and exhibits excellent anti-clogging and anti-smearing properties. The luminescent films on the other hand are characterized by good mechanical strength (σUTS ≈ 61.3 MPa) along with high luminescence efficiency. The luminescence yield, ultimate tensile stress, hydrophobicity and glass transition temperature of the films were found to scale similarly with the concentration of CDs in chitosan. All the parameters initially improved with increasing CD concentration but then deteriorated beyond some optimal CD loading due to agglomeration effect. We demonstrate that the amorphous carbon dot-based inks and films outperform all other carbon-based fluorescent inks and films prepared from the more expensive crystalline structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Invisible ink
Fluorescent films

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural features of amorphous CDs and CD-chitosan composites
Absorption and emission characteristics: some interesting indication on mechanism of light emission

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology, Shibpur

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful to Dr. Niladri Banerjee of University of Loughborough, UK for stimulating discussions and careful scrutiny of the manuscript. We all thank Professors Tarun Mondal and Sugata Ray and Mr. Indranath Bhowmik of IACS, Kolkata for extending their support to record XPS data of our samples and we thank Mr. Pravin Sawai for helping us with the thermal measurements. ABS acknowledges the support of IIEST, Shibpur PhD fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the preparation of amorphous CD-chitosan composite based fluorescent inks and luminescent films starting from the ultrasonic synthesis of amorphous CDs.

Structural features of the as-synthesized amorphous CDs and CD-chitosan composite inks and films. (a) Bright field HR-TEM image of the amorphous CDs; (b) the corresponding SAEDP; (c) high magnification image showing an isolated CD (no signature of any fringe); (d) XRD profile of the CD powder; (e) TEM image of the CD-chitosan composite ink; (f) low-magnification image of dehydrated CD-chitosan film for CDF10 sample (the dark lines along the top and left corner is a section of the TEM grid); and (g) high magnification image of CDF10 showing larger dots due to agglomeration.

(a) Elemental map of a CD showing self-passivation of clusters of carbon atoms with C, H, O and their compounds. Left to right: elemental maps of C, H, O and a composite map of C, H, O obtained by EF-TEM measurements. (b) XPS wide scan spectrum of the synthesized CDs; (c) C1s; and (d) O1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared CDs.
FTIR spectrum of CDs, CDs-Chitosan composite based ink and films.

UV–visible absorption and PL from CDs in DI water and mixture of CDs and chitosan solution. (a) Co-plot of UV–visible absorption spectrum and excitation dependent PL spectra of the as-prepared CDs in DI water; (b) UV–vis and PL characteristics of the CD-chitosan colloidal composite; and (c) photograph showing intense room temperature blue-green-red emission obtained by exciting the CD solution with 350, 400 and 450 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Images showing luminescence under UV irradiation from CD-chitosan ink-based writings on a variety of substrates like aluminium tape, ceramic plate, silicon wafer, black art paper, white tissue paper, transparency sheet and glass slide. Photographs of the corresponding substrates under ambient light are also shown. Practically the visibility is zero under normal lighting except for scratch marks due to the pen which are visible on the Al, Si and tissue substrates. A photograph of the CD filled pen under UV illumination is shown at the bottom centre. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

CD-Chitosan based florescent films. The blank film with no CDs shows no observable PL and the film with high loading of CD (CDF10) also shows a drastic PL quenching due to agglomeration leading to concentration quenching effect.

(a) Stress-strain curve of the CD-chitosan films for different concentration of CD loading. Inset shows the variation of ultimate tensile stress ( σ
UTS) for different CD concentration in the different films; (b) variation of average contact angles and (c) variation of T g of the films.

head.ce:title: Amorphous carbon dot and chitosan based composites as fluorescent inks and luminescent films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. M. N. Dastur School of Materials Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology, Shibpur, P.O.: Botanic Garden, Howrah, 711103, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. M. N. Dastur School of Materials Science and Engineering
Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dr. M. N. Dastur School of Materials Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology, Shibpur, P.O.: Botanic Garden, Howrah, 711103, West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Metallurgical Laboratory, Jamshedpur, 831007, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering and Sciences, Tecnológico de Monterrey, Monterrey, 64849, Nuevo Leon, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Sciences, Tecnologico de Monterrey, Monterrey, 64849, Nuevo Leon, Mexico

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Engineering and Sciences, Tecnológico de Monterrey, Monterrey, 64849, Nuevo Leon, Mexico.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A composite of self-passivated amorphous carbon dots (CDs) and chitosan has been developed and utilized to form fluorescent inks and luminescent films. The ink is invisible under visible light but glows brightly under external excitation. Cross-linking between the numerous surface groups present in the highly disordered CDs and chitosan, endow the inks and films with enhanced optical and mechanical properties. The amorphous CD based ink is capable of writing on nearly all types of surfaces and exhibits excellent anti-clogging and anti-smearing properties. The luminescent films on the other hand are characterized by good mechanical strength (σUTS ≈ 61.3 MPa) along with high luminescence efficiency. The luminescence yield, ultimate tensile stress, hydrophobicity and glass transition temperature of the films were found to scale similarly with the concentration of CDs in chitosan. All the parameters initially improved with increasing CD concentration but then deteriorated beyond some optimal CD loading due to agglomeration effect. We demonstrate that the amorphous carbon dot-based inks and films outperform all other carbon-based fluorescent inks and films prepared from the more expensive crystalline structures.

ce:section.ce:para: Amorphous carbon dots (CDs) have received much less attention compared to their more glamorous crystalline counterparts like carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Like the carbon based QDs, these quasi zero-dimensional amorphous cluster of carbon atoms are water soluble and bio-compatible [1,2]. However, unlike the crystalline variants, the amorphous dots can be easily synthesized in bulk quantities from the abundant sources of carbon like glucose using simple and inexpensive techniques [3]. Due to the amorphous nature, these CDs are robust, behave isotropically with respect to mechanical properties and are predicted to have graphite-like properties, while having hardness like diamond [4]. Interestingly, experiments show that the optical characteristics of amorphous CDs are very similar to the CQDs or GQDs in nearly all aspect of spectroscopic properties, including excitation energy tuneable visible emission [2,3]. Consequently, these apparently mundane nanostructures are expected to have various applications involving their luminescence and mechanical properties, although very little has so far been explored [3].

In this work we show that a composite of amorphous CDs and chitosan can be used to prepare fluorescent inks and luminescent films. The inks and the films thus prepared, have properties which are unmatched by the inks and films prepared from the crystalline allotropes of carbon. Unlike the luminescent inks reported so far, the amorphous CD-chitosan based inks can write on nearly all types of surfaces including metals, polymers, ceramics, glasses, silicon wafers and papers and exhibit smear and clog resistance that is better than a commercially available pen. The films, formed by simple dehydration of the amorphous CD-chitosan composite, are characterized by very high luminescence yield along with a yield strength of 61.3 MPa.

Fluorescent inks are used as “security marks” that are invisible under visible light but becomes distinct when excited with UV radiation of suitable wavelength. They can be used for multicolour barcodes to authenticate frequently counterfeited products and for information storage [5]. The commercially available fluorescent inks based on dyes or pigments have certain limitations. First, the amount of the colouring pigment needs to be delicately balanced, since the margin between the amount of dye needed for reasonable fluorescence intensity and the amount wherein the dye molecules begin to aggregate and quench fluorescence due to “concentration quenching”, is very narrow [6,7]. Secondly, they are limited by poor photostability and small stokes shift [8]. Thirdly, most of the commercially available dye based inks involve toxic chemicals and are consequently unsuitable for biological applications [9]. In response to the disadvantages of conventional dye based inks, some carbon nanostructure (primarily crystalline CD, like the GQD) based fluorescent inks have been investigated in recent times [1,5,10–14]. The CD based inks are preferred because of their stable fluorescence, low-toxicity, inexpensive preparation strategies, large stokes shift and excitation dependent emission [10–14]. The excitation dependent emission of the CDs resulting in different optical outputs in response to different external stimuli, endows them with enhanced security features that are difficult to mimic [5].

Although, the CQD and GQD based inks have reported much better performance than the commercially available dye-based inks, there are four major issues that have restricted their widespread use. First, nearly all the carbon nanostructure based inks reported so far, to our knowledge, can be used to write only on specific substrates [15,16]. Second, smearing, smudging and clogging of fluorescent inks are commonly encountered problems with CD based inks, but have been seldom discussed in the scientific literature [17]. Third, information coded with some carbon-based inks are often visible under ambient light [13,17,18]. Finally, nearly all reported preparation methods are relatively complicated and involve expensive reagents/techniques when compared with the commercially available dyes (see Table S1 in the supporting information for a detailed comparison).

The amorphous CD-chitosan based inks discussed here addresses all the above issues. The preparation of the CD-chitosan composites, starting from the preparation of the amorphous CDs is simple and inexpensive [3]. The as-prepared ink can write on nearly all types of substrates – metals, polymers, ceramics, wafers, papers and glass slides. Besides having very high QY and excellent stability, these inks show exceptional resistance to clogging, smearing and smudging which allows them to print details.

The same amorphous CD-chitosan composites were simply dehydrated to form bio-compatible fluorescent films with tuneable mechanical strength. Most importantly, the films combine high fluorescence yield with excellent mechanical properties which has so far been a major challenge. Enhanced crosslinking in the CD-chitosan composites resulted in enhanced mechanical strength, hydrophobicity and glass transition temperature, which increased with increasing CD concentration up to some optimal CD loading. Beyond this optimal concentration all the parameters deteriorated due to agglomeration effect. Such films have wide applications, ranging from electronic displays to drug encapsulation. Quantitative comparison of the performance of the amorphous CD-chitosan based inks and the films in terms of luminescence yield, stability, mechanical properties and other performance metrics, clearly reveal that these inks and films outperform all other inks and films (mainly, GQD based) that have been reported in literature.

The preparation of amorphous CDs involves simple ultrasonication (Piezo U-Sonic) of a 1:1 (volume ratio) mixture of 1 M dextrose solution and HCl. With increasing time of ultrasonication the solution changes from colourless to brown. After 12 h of sonication the brown coloured solution was oven-dried at 80 °C under ambient pressure for 24 h to obtain a dark brown coloured dry powder. This powder was dissolved in DI water and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The light yellowish supernatant was separated and found to contain fine dispersion of water-soluble, amorphous CDs.

In the next step, 500 mg of chitosan was dissolved in 50 mL DI water by simple stirring. An aliquot of 500 μL of acetic acid was added to the mixture in order to polymerize chitosan. The mixture was stirred until chitosan fully dissolved, i.e. till a visibly clear solution was obtained. Subsequently, the desired amount of CD dispersion was added to it and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. This led to the formation of a colourless gel, which was ready for use as fluorescent ink. For the sake of comparison, chitosan ink without CDs was also made separately.

The fine dispersion supernatant was separated after centrifuging the ultrasonicated 10 mg/mL amorphous CDs in DI water. Different volume of this dispersion was poured into the solution formed by mixing 100 mg of chitosan and 100 μL of acetic acid in 10 mL DI water. The different volume of CD dispersions taken were 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 mL, respectively to form CD based films labelled as CDF1, CDF2, CDF3, CDF5 and CDF10. A film was made of chitosan only without any CD. The resulting gels formed after thorough mixing, were poured into plastic petri dishes and were dried for 24 h under a 60 W tungsten lamp. This led to the formation of fluorescent flexible films which could be easily separated from petri dish with tweezers after complete drying. Following earlier reports on the influence of alkali post-treatment of chitosan-based films, the dried films were washed with 1 M NaOH solution for 30 min in an attempt to improve the physicochemical and mechanical properties of the films [19,20]. Finally, the films were washed thoroughly with DI water and further dried for 24 h for different characterizations.

Investigation into the crystalline nature of the synthesized CDs was carried out by XRD using a Brucker D8 advanced diffractometer operating at 40 kV, using Cu Kα
1 (λ = 1.54056 Å) radiation. Structure of the samples was investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) using a JEOL JEM-2200 FS TEM equipped with a 200 kV field emission gun and in-column energy filter (Omega) along with Gatan software. The samples were five times diluted prior to deposition and 2 μL of samples were deposited on carbon coated cupper grids for HR-TEM measurements. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Jasco 4700 LE FTIR spectrometer. XPS measurements were conducted on an Omicron Multiprobe (Omicron NanoTechnology Gmbh, UK) spectrometer fitted with an EA125 (Omicron) hemispherical analyzer. Monochromatic Al-K
α source operated at 150 W was used and the pass energy of the analyzer was kept at 40 eV. A low-energy electron gun (SL1000, Omicron) with a large spot size was used for sample neutralization. The voltage of the electron gun was fixed at −3 V. UV–visible spectroscopy was performed using a JASCO V-750, UV–vis spectrophotometer. Steady state PL property of different samples was investigated using a Horiba Jobin Yvon, Fluorolog-3 (Nanolog) spectrofluorometer (model FL3-11) fitted with a 450 W xenon lamp source, photomultiplier tube detector and single grating monochromator. During observation, entry and exit slit widths were kept at 1 nm and the integrating time was 0.5 s. Absolute PL QY of the samples were estimated by recording the PL spectra using an integrating sphere following the method proposed by Pålsson and Monkman [21], and de Mello et al. [22] The integrating sphere was mounted inside the Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer and the samples were mounted into the holder inside the sphere. The measured spectra were background corrected by recording a blank data. The tensile testing of the films was done using Tinius Olsen 5 ST. The width of the samples was 20 mm and thickness was 0.02 mm (measured with digital Vernier calliper). Contact angle was measured using a Surface tensiometer SURFTENS 4.5 and DSC data were recorded by a Netzsch DSC-200 PC Phoenix, Germany.

The entire recipe for the formation of the CD-chitosan based inks and films starting from the synthesis of amorphous CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The plausible mechanism of formation of amorphous CDs by ultrasonication of dextrose in HCL has been discussed before [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural features of amorphous CDs and CD-chitosan composites
ce:section.ce:para: Chemically assisted ultrasonic treatment of dextrose followed by centrifugation and drying produces well-dispersed localized clusters of carbon atoms as depicted in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) micrographs shown in Fig. 2 a [and in Fig. S1a in the supporting information]. The diameters of these roughly spherical domains vary between ~0.5 and 5.5 nm ± 0.6 nm (supporting information). The amorphous character of the CDs is evident from the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAEDP) shown in Fig. 2b, the high magnification bright field image shown in Fig. 2c, and the x-ray diffraction (XRD) profile shown in Fig. 2d. The SAEDP consists of a broad diffused halo that is typical of an amorphous sample. There is no sign of any diffraction reinforced feature. Crystalline fringes are clearly absent in the high magnification image capturing a single large dot [Figs. 2c and S1b]. The XRD profile of the CDs (obtained by drying the CD colloids in vacuum dryer), comprises of a very broad and diffused hump extending from 2θ ≈ 18 to 38° and peaking at 2θ = 26°, which is a signature of amorphous carbon [23]. The size, shape and distribution of the CDs remain unaffected after mixing with chitosan. A typical TEM image of the amorphous CD-chitosan colloidal composite [Fig. 2e] looks similar to the one shown in Fig. 2a. However, when the solutions are left undisturbed and allowed to dehydrate for the formation of films, the CDs tend to agglomerate forming larger clusters of carbon atoms. Fig. 2f and g are the bright field TEM images of CD-chitosan dehydrated films for sample CDF10. Although we find reasonably well distributed dark spots representing the CDs in the low-magnification image shown in Fig. 2f, the average size of the clusters clearly increases as a result of agglomeration as evidenced from the high magnification image shown in Fig. 2g for the CDF10 sample.

The procedure adopted for synthesis of the CDs does not require any additional specie to passivate or functionalize the surfaces of the synthesized CDs. The amorphous clusters of carbon atoms are self-passivated with H, O and functional groups involving C, H and O. Elemental maps of C, H and O obtained by energy filtered TEM (EF-TEM) measurements are shown in Fig. 3 a, wherein, we clearly see clustering of carbon atoms along with distribution of H and O on the surface of the cluster. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements provide insight about the composition and surface groups present in the CDs. The wide scan XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3b exhibits two strong peaks at 285.5 and 532.5 eV which are due to C and O, respectively [24]. The C1s spectrum de-convolutes into four components corresponding to CC (sp2 carbon), C–C (sp3 carbon), C-OR and COOR, peaking at 283.2, 284.7, 285.4 and 289.5 eV, respectively [Fig. 3c] [25]. The O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3d, has three components at 529.6, 531.1 and 532 eV, respectively, due to the CO, C–OH and C–O–C groups [26,27]. Therefore, the EF-TEM and XPS are in mutual agreement and allow us to identify the functional groups passivating the clusters of carbon atoms. Overall, the structural and compositional characteristics confirm the formation of self-passivated amorphous CDs. We have recently discussed the mechanism of formation of such disordered domains of carbon atoms starting from any substance having C, H and O, in the ratio 1:2:1 [3].

The specific functional groups present in the CDs and after incorporating into the chitosan matrix to form ink and film are identified from the Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) analysis shown in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectrum confirms the carbonization of dextrose into self-passivated CDs, as it displays several peaks related to the surface bonds present in the CDs. As per the region of interest we observed that in CDs there is carbonyl functional groups (CO) bands peaking at 1704 cm−1and 710 cm−1 [28], that do not appear in the FTIR spectra of CDs-chitosan film or ink. This is quite evident to the fact that aldehyde groups are consumed and responsible for crosslinking in chitosan [29,30]. The FTIR spectra of films clearly reveals the signature peaks of chitosan film at 1566 cm−1 corresponds to imine (-CN) bonds which is formed by crosslinking reaction between aldehyde (-CO) groups in the CDs and amino (-NH2) groups in the chitosan [31]. This signature peak also confirms the formation of Schiff base after the crosslinking reaction. Moreover, it is apparently visible that this peak becomes stronger and sharper with increasing the concentration of CDs up to CDF 5, but become comparatively weaker for CDF 10. However, there is an absorption peak at 1656 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibration of the remaining (after crosslinking reaction) acetamide (-CONH) groups in chitosan film [32]. There is another peak at 1050 cm−1 corresponds to ether (-O-) groups in the CDs spectrum [28]. This peak is also observed in the films and found to be getting stronger with respect to increase in CDs concentration, suggesting participation in making ether chain linkage in the crosslinking. As reported by Uragami et al., [33] besides aldehyde groups, ether groups is also able to cross-link chitosan molecules by anchoring two epoxy groups on two amino groups respectively in different chitosan chains. In the FTIR spectra of ink there is no sign of crosslinking when it is in liquid form, but there is very sharp peak of acetamide (-CONH) groups compared to films, which is basic potential entity in crosslinking Schiff base reaction. So, this ink is more likely to get crosslinked as soon as it gets dehydrated while writing, as found in the FTIR spectra of films. In addition to all functional groups discussed, there are few other functional groups in CDs, film and ink which are identified and marked on Fig. 4. The FTIR spectrum therefore consolidates the fact that the surfaces of carbon clusters are passivated by functional groups that are derived during the carbonization process of dextrose and these surface groups are utilized to cross-link the chitosan to make florescent film, and remain in acetamide form to be utilized as ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and emission characteristics: some interesting indication on mechanism of light emission
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the optical absorption and emission characteristics of aqueous dispersions of amorphous CDs are remarkably similar to the crystalline CQDs and GQDs. A typical UV–visible spectrum of aqueous suspension of the CDs, shown in Fig. 5 a, consists of two notable absorption features – a high energy peak at ~228 nm along with a lower energy shoulder centred at ~332 nm. Like all other variants of carbon nanodots, the sample exhibits strong absorption in the UV region and has an absorption onset around 450 nm. The PL spectra of the CDs show dominant emission peaks in the blue-green region, which can be tuned by changing the excitation energy as shown in Fig. 5a. The absolute QY of the as-prepared CDs was found to have a maximum value of ~39.5% in the blue region corresponding to 330 nm UV excitation.

When the aqueous CD suspensions are simply added to the chitosan solution and stirred to prepare the CD-chitosan based inks or films, the intensity of emission increases significantly, which can be detected even on visual inspection (Fig. S2, supporting information). The calculated absolute QY increased to ~51% for the CD-chitosan colloidal composite as compared to the QY of ~39.5% for the as-synthesized amorphous CD colloid for 330 nm excitation. Furthermore, we observe from Fig. 5b that the shift in emission peak with changing excitation energy, i.e. the excitation dependency of the emission energy decreases in comparison with the aqueous CDs. As the excitation energy is varied from 350 to 450 nm, the emission peak energy shits from ~448 to 502 nm in case of the CD-chitosan composite, whereas, for the same change in excitation the shift in emission peak is from 447 to 522 nm for the CDs in water. In fact, for the CD-chitosan sample, initially the emission peak remains almost invariant with excitation till 400 nm and then exhibits a red-shift with decreasing excitation energy. The intensity of emission in case of CDs in water almost monotonically decreases with decreasing excitation energy, whereas the intensity of the CD-chitosan initially increases and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. Also, what appeared as a weak shoulder in the absorption profiles of the CDs at 332 nm now manifests as a dominant peak centred at ~344 nm in the absorption spectrum of the CD-chitosan composite. Images of the excitation dependent emission from luminescent colloids exhibiting blue-green-red emissions under different excitation energies are shown in Fig. 5c.

Although a substantial amount of work has been done on light emitting carbon nanostructures, the mechanism of luminescence from such carbonaceous nanomaterials has hitherto remained controversial [34,35]. Initial studies on mechanism of PL from CDs focused primarily on the quantum confinement effect [36,37]. Later works shifted more towards the dominant role of emissive traps and surface groups [23,38]. Recent reports have attributed the emission and absorption features to molecular fluorophores and polycyclic hydrocarbon domains formed at different stages of synthesis of the CDs [35]. In this communication, we will not delve deep into the mechanism of PL, as this topic warrants a separate and detailed analysis. Here, we will primarily note the striking similarities of the absorption and emission spectra of the crystalline carbon nanostructures, amorphous CDs, and the CD-chitosan composites. The following features are readily identifiable: (i) two peaks/shoulders in the UV region (one within ~200–240 nm and another within ~300–350 nm) of the absorption profile, (ii) excitation dependent PL emission (under certain situations) and (iii) a large Stokes shift. Structurally, however, the differences between the crystalline and amorphous dots are more pronounced than the similarities. The common entities that are expected to be present in the crystalline and amorphous CDs and in the CD-chitosan composites, are the five or six-membered heterocyclic carbon rings — typically the aromatic hydrocarbons. Abundance of such heterocyclic carbon structures in our samples is confirmed by the XPS and FTIR measurements.

Such carbogenic structures are present in nearly all variants of CDs. They may be present as free molecules or on the surface of the nanostructures or may be incorporated within the core of a CD [39]. Our findings, so far, tend to reinforce the role of these heterocyclic carbon structures in the light emission mechanism observed in CD systems. The enhancement of PL when the CDs are mixed with chitosan originates from the cross-linked heterocyclic hydrocarbon units. In fact, cross-linking of polymeric units in general has been reported to enhance emission from fluorescent centres or fluorophores [29,40]. The carbonyl groups present in the synthesized CDs bond with chitosan and enhance crosslinking (FTIR analysis). The passivation of excitation dependent surface groups of the CDs, on the one hand, decreases excitation dependence, while on the other hand, increases PL yield due to the crosslinking enhanced emission effect [40]. Since the surface states are expected to be present inside the widened composite bandgap of clusters of carbon atoms, passivation of these states due to crosslinking also explains the observed excitation independence in the CD-chitosan sample at higher energies. Understandably, when the excitation energy falls below the bandgap, the emission peak once again becomes excitation energy dependent due to dominant transitions via the surface states present inside the bandgap [23]. The enhanced absorption at ~344 nm (Fig. 5b) along with ~40 nm red-shift of the higher energy absorption peak also relates well with such crosslinking effect.

The mixture of CDs and chitosan was transferred inside an empty refill of a commercially available gel pen and used as writing ink. Some images of the patterns written on different substrates are shown in Fig. 6 . What makes this amorphous CD based ink distinctly different from all other forms of fluorescent inks is their remarkable ability to write on practically any surface. As shown in Fig. 6, we attempted the following substrates – metal (Al tape), ceramic (a silica crucible), polymer (commercially available transparency sheet), silicon wafer, standard glass slide and different kinds of papers including tissue paper. The ability of the CD-chitosan ink to write on different types of materials is an important attribute, since this feature would allow using this ink for printing holograms on a variety of materials to prevent counterfeiting. To the best of our knowledge, there is no existing report of any invisible fluorescent ink that is capable of printing on substrates ranging from metal to ceramic to polymers (please see Table S1, supporting information for details). Besides their ability to print on a wide range of substrates, we would like to highlight two other notable features of the amorphous CD-chitosan based ink. First, the ink exhibits excellent smear resistance. In fact, we could easily print details on a tissue paper, on which nearly all forms of available gel-based inks would usually smear and smudge. A simple smear test was performed by comparing the smearing of the CD-chitosan based ink with that of a commercially available Parker roller gel pen. The smudge test clearly revealed that the CD based ink has better smear resistance. A video of the smudge test is provided as a supplementary video file (Video: ESV1). Secondly, the ink made from these CDs was found to be stable even after six months under ambient conditions — no sign of clogging was observed.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2020.122984

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
video.1
1
video.1
video 2
2
video 2

All the above attributes of the CD-chitosan ink can be easily understood in terms of enhanced crosslinking between chitosan and the disordered CDs. Due to the disordered amorphous character, the CDs have more abundant availability of sites for crosslinking, as compared to the crystalline counterparts. Consequently, enhanced crosslinking with chitosan is an expected outcome which in turn endows the CD-chitosan ink with all the remarkable properties. On the one hand, crosslinking of chitosan with the CDs inhibits smearing of the ink due to the adhesive quality of chitosan. On the other hand, a low surface tension and a high dispersive part allows chitosan based amorphous CD colloids to easily spread and adhere on almost every type of materials. These properties of chitosan based solutions have been reported earlier and utilized in a number of applications [41]. Finally, the same crosslinking of the hydrophilic surface groups of the amorphous CDs with chitosan is expected to lower the surface tension of the colloidal composites, which in turn provide the necessary condition to keep the CDs in colloidal suspension without agglomeration or clogging.

Transparent florescent films labelled as CDF1, CDF2, CDF3, CDF5 and CDF10 were prepared by drying the colloidal solutions of amorphous CD and chitosan under a 60 W tungsten lamp. A bare chitosan film was also prepared similarly. The appearance of these films under ambient light and under UV are shown in Fig. 7 a and b, respectively, and the transparency data is presented in Fig. S3 in the supporting information. The film containing only chitosan and no CDs does not exhibit any detectable fluorescence, while the PL intensity of the films made out of the composites of chitosan and amorphous CDs initially appear to increase with increase in the CD content but then decreases sharply when the CD content is further increased. Drastic fall in intensity for large loading is expected due to the well-known concentration-quenching effect [42]. But we see that for the amorphous, self-passivated CDs reported here, there is a large margin (CDF1 to CDF5) between the amount needed to obtain substantial fluorescence and the amount beyond which the fluorescent intensity starts quenching due to agglomeration.

Due to the excellent biological attributes of chitosan and since it can be easily formed into structures under mild processing conditions, many researchers have extensively investigated the material as well as its composites with other non-toxic materials [43,44]. In fact, carbon nanostructure (mostly crystalline) and chitosan based composites have been investigated in recent times which can be broadly grouped under two types (see Table S2 in supporting information for details). One, composite films with high fluorescence yield — primarily made out of the more expensive crystalline CDs, but with inferior or un-reported mechanical properties and/or wettability or swelling ratios [15,45–48]. Two, films with reasonably good strength and other mechanical properties, but with either no or very feeble luminescence [45,49–51]. However, a state-of-the-art film is expected to combine both the attributes — high fluorescence yield coupled with good mechanical properties, besides being bio-compatible. Therefore, to investigate the mechanical properties of the films, tensile tests were performed with universal testing machine (UTM) on films with diameter 20 mm and thickness 0.02 mm. The stress-strain curves for the different films are shown in Fig. 8 a and the variation of tensile strength ( σ
UTS) with CD concentration is shown as inset, Fig. 8a. We clearly see that with the addition of CDs the tensile strength of the films increases from 28.6 MPa for bare chitosan film to 61.3 MPa for CDF5. However, the strength drops to 38.3 MPa for the CDF 10 film. Such a variation of tensile strength with CD concentration relates well with the crosslinking effect [52], which is also the reason for enhanced emission from CD-chitosan composite. We have seen from the FTIR and XPS results that there are many surface functional groups present in the CDs, which link via covalent linkages or hydrogen bonds with the chitosan molecules leading to the crosslinking effect. Earlier studies have revealed that strengthening observed in polymer films having nanosized inclusions involve the crosslinking of polymer chains between neighbouring particulates rather than depending on load transfer mechanisms operative in composites reinforced with micrometre-sized inorganic fibres [53,54]. The cross-linked bonds inhibit the propagation of cracks and increases the overall energy required for rupturing material [55]. Such effects are expectedly much more dominant in the amorphous variants of carbon nanostructures due to the omnipresence of a cross-linked architecture. As discussed earlier, the overall disordered character promotes enhanced linkages with chitosan forming a thoroughly cross-linked composite. It is due to this fact the tensile strength of the amorphous CD based films is much better than most other luminescent films reported so far, which were based mostly on crystalline nanostructures (Table S2 in supporting information). To highlight the fact that the improved mechanical property of the films is not only due to chitosan, but due to the amorphous nature of the CDs, we have separately compared the performance of these films with other chitosan and carbon nanostructure-based films. From Table S2, we see that chitosan-carbon nanotube (unzipped) composite films have better mechanical properties, but they are not luminescent.

We also note that the increase in mechanical strength of the CD-chitosan composite films is dependent on the concentration of CD loading. The variation of σ
UTS with CD concentration of our films, shown as inset Fig. 8a, shows that with the addition of CDs the tensile strength of the films increases for chitosan film to CDF5, and then it drops for the CDF10 film In general the mechanical strength of a polymer based nanocomposite is expected to increase with the density of crosslinking [56–58]. However, in our case an increase in CD concentration beyond certain optimal loading (CDF10) leads to agglomeration of the CDs as evidenced from the TEM micrographs shown in Fig. 2. Since, crosslinking is dominantly mediated by surface linkages between the CDs and chitosan, it is reasonable to expect that agglomeration would reduce the density of crosslinking thereby having a negative impact on the mechanical strength. Consequently, mechanical strength of the CD-chitosan composite should deteriorate when CDs agglomerate. Similar deterioration of mechanical strength of nanocomposite based polymer films at higher concentration of nano-inclusions have also been previously reported [52,55].

The negative impact of excessive loading leading to aggregation of CDs is not only limited to mechanical strength but and also manifested and consistent with the variation of average contact angle shown in Fig. 8b and glass transition temperature (T g) shown in Fig. 8c (DSC curves are shown in Fig. S4 of the supporting information). Both, contact angle and T g scale similarly with σ
UTS, providing additional support towards the enhanced crosslinking effect. Initially, as the polar surfaces of the CDs are passivated in a chitosan matrix, the average contact angle, i.e. the hydrophobicity increases. But beyond certain concentration, increase in CD loading creates CD agglomerates which affect a deterioration of hydrophobicity and strength. Similarly, T g increases initially due to optimal crosslinking till a certain loading (CDF5), following which we see a decrease in the glass transition temperature (Fig. 8c). It is a known fact that the T g of chitosan based film is not constant but it varies as per the water content of the film [59]. The film with less amount of water will have comparatively higher T g value, which is also true for hydrophobicity. So, the contact angles and T g are expected to exhibit similar trends with respect to variation of CDs content. Along with intense PL, mechanical strength and relatively high glass transition temperature render these films attractive for multipurpose applications, particularly in luminescent packaging of drugs for tracked and targeted delivery.

In summary, we have developed a new class of fluorescent inks and luminescent films based on self-passivated, amorphous CDs dispersed in chitosan matrix. Due to the amorphous nature of the CDs, both the inks and the films have properties that are superior compared to inks and films produced by crystalline carbon nanostructures. A simple mixture of CDs with chitosan produces fluorescent inks with high QY (c.a. 51%). The inks remain stable for several months and exhibit resistance to clogging and smudging. Most importantly, this ink is capable of printing on a variety of surfaces. The same amorphous CDs mixed with chitosan can be used to prepare luminescent films with excellent mechanical and thermal properties which are believed to be the fallout of crosslinking effect. Interestingly, the PL yield, mechanical strength, hydrophobicity and glass transition temperature — all scale similarly with concentration of CDs in chitosan — initially exhibit a rise in their values with increasing CD concentration but then decreases beyond some optimal value of CD loading due to agglomeration effect. These water dispersible and bio-degradable fluorescent inks and films have immense potential applications, particularly in the field of biology.

Supporting Information containing the following: i) Comparison of the optical properties of carbon nanostructure based fluorescent inks; ii) Additional electron micrographs and size distribution of the CDs; iii) pictures of luminescence CD-chitosan composite; iv) FTIR of the samples; v) transmittance study of the films; vi) comparison of the performance of different carbon nanostructure based films; vii) DSC of the fluorescent films, are available with this article.

ABS and MR conceived, planned and designed the experiments. ABS performed the synthesis and the optical, mechanical and thermal characterization. VPS assisted ABS in sample preparation and independently prepared a second set of samples and characterized. AKP did the electron microscopy. ABS and MR analyzed the results and drafted the manuscript. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to Dr. Niladri Banerjee of University of Loughborough, UK for stimulating discussions and careful scrutiny of the manuscript. We all thank Professors Tarun Mondal and Sugata Ray and Mr. Indranath Bhowmik of IACS, Kolkata for extending their support to record XPS data of our samples and we thank Mr. Pravin Sawai for helping us with the thermal measurements. ABS acknowledges the support of IIEST, Shibpur PhD fellowship.
